title: [BUG] addImage to single cell with floating point has wrong behaviour
author: amay077

comments.author: ShawnZeng
comments.body:
I have same issue. It seems it happened after I set a height for the row and use floating point, the floating point is calculated by the old height. 

I set height just before adding image.
`          let tempRow = workSheet.getRow(j+2)
           tempRow.height = EXCEL_IMAGE_CELL_HEIGHT
`
Add image using floating point:
`   
          workSheet.addImage(alldata[j,i,0].ImageID,{
            tl: { col: i, row: j+1 },
            br: { col: colEnd, row: rowEnd }   // rowEnd  is 1.75
          })`

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29124100/128618588-e89c68d9-7f3b-4c3b-a73d-51f95e39f9cb.png)

comments.author: HensCoderoad
comments.body: same issue, Does anyone else have a solution？

comments.author: stringthread
comments.body:
Hello! I encountered the same issue and after investigating, I identified the cause of the bug.

The cause of this bug is incorrect unit conversion to calculate the fractional part of the coordinates (\<xdr:colOff\>, \<xdr:rowOff\>).

In [lib/doc/anchor.js@L62](https://github.com/exceljs/exceljs/blob/5bed18b45e824f409b08456b59b87430ded023ab/lib/doc/anchor.js#L62), the column widths (font-dependent) should be converted to EMUs (English Metric Units). However, they are currently being multiplied by 10,000 instead.
As a result, in my environment, the column offset (Anchor.nativeColOff), which should be 321,945 EMUs, ends up being only 22,650 EMUs, approximately 1/14th of the correct value.

This Microsoft document explains how to convert columns to pixels, which can be used to determine the correct multiplication factor for the offset:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/office/developer/office-2010/cc802410(v=office.14)

However, it’s not feasible to specify `{br: {col: 1.9}}` as you intended, since the offset needs to be a value less than 1.

The best solution might be to fork the repository and modify the multiplication factor at [lib/doc/anchor.js@L62](https://github.com/exceljs/exceljs/blob/5bed18b45e824f409b08456b59b87430ded023ab/lib/doc/anchor.js#L62)z. While adjusting ExcelJS itself could be challenging due to its dependency on font settings, forking and making the modification for personal use is relatively straightforward.

I hope this information helps you.
